section_text_parts: You could have a sequence of data that you would like to send in a " stream ", you could do it with JSON Lines .
roots.section_text: You could have a sequence of data that you would like to send in a " stream ", you could do it with JSON Lines .

section_text_parts: " Streaming " data means that your app will start sending data items to the client without waiting for the entire sequence of items to be ready.
So, it will send the first item, the client will receive and start processing it, and you might still be producing the next item.
It could even be an infinite stream, where you keep sending data.
children.section_text:
" Streaming " data means that your app will start sending data items to the client without waiting for the entire sequence of items to be ready.

So, it will send the first item, the client will receive and start processing it, and you might still be producing the next item.

It could even be an infinite stream, where you keep sending data.

code_blocks.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant App
    participant Client

    App->>App: Produce Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 1
    App->>App: Produce Item 2
    Client->>Client: Process Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 2
    App->>App: Produce Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 2
    App->>Client: Send Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 3
    Note over App: Keeps producing...
    Note over Client: Keeps consuming...

section_text_parts: In these cases, it's common to send " JSON Lines ", which is a format where you send one JSON object per line.
A response would have a content type of application/jsonl (instead of application/json ) and the body would be something like:

It's very similar to a JSON array (equivalent of a Python list), but instead of being wrapped in [] and having , between the items, it has one JSON object per line , they are separated by a new line character.
children.section_text:
In these cases, it's common to send " JSON Lines ", which is a format where you send one JSON object per line.

A response would have a content type of application/jsonl (instead of application/json ) and the body would be something like:

It's very similar to a JSON array (equivalent of a Python list), but instead of being wrapped in [] and having , between the items, it has one JSON object per line , they are separated by a new line character.

section_text_parts: You could use this to stream data from an AI LLM service, from logs or telemetry , or from other types of data that can be structured in JSON items.
children.section_text: You could use this to stream data from an AI LLM service, from logs or telemetry , or from other types of data that can be structured in JSON items.

section_text_parts: To stream JSON Lines with FastAPI you can, instead of using return in your path operation function , use yield to produce each item in turn.
If each JSON item you want to send back is of type Item (a Pydantic model) and it's an async function, you can declare the return type as AsyncIterable[Item] :
If you declare the return type, FastAPI will use it to validate the data, document it in OpenAPI, filter it, and serialize it using Pydantic.
children.section_text:
To stream JSON Lines with FastAPI you can, instead of using return in your path operation function , use yield to produce each item in turn.

If each JSON item you want to send back is of type Item (a Pydantic model) and it's an async function, you can declare the return type as AsyncIterable[Item] :

If you declare the return type, FastAPI will use it to validate the data, document it in OpenAPI, filter it, and serialize it using Pydantic.

section_text_parts: You can also use regular def functions (without async ), and use yield the same way.
FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.
As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be Iterable[Item] :
children.section_text:
You can also use regular def functions (without async ), and use yield the same way.

FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.

As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be Iterable[Item] :

section_text_parts: You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will then use the jsonable_encoder to convert the data to something that can be serialized to JSON and then send it as JSON Lines.
children.section_text: You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will then use the jsonable_encoder to convert the data to something that can be serialized to JSON and then send it as JSON Lines.

section_text_parts: FastAPI also has first-class support for Server-Sent Events (SSE), which are quite similar but with a couple of extra details. You can learn about them in the next chapter: Server-Sent Events (SSE) . 🤓
children.section_text: FastAPI also has first-class support for Server-Sent Events (SSE), which are quite similar but with a couple of extra details. You can learn about them in the next chapter: Server-Sent Events (SSE) . 🤓

flat_nodes.section_text: You could have a sequence of data that you would like to send in a " stream ", you could do it with JSON Lines .

" Streaming " data means that your app will start sending data items to the client without waiting for the entire sequence of items to be ready.

So, it will send the first item, the client will receive and start processing it, and you might still be producing the next item.

It could even be an infinite stream, where you keep sending data.

code_blocks.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant App
    participant Client

    App->>App: Produce Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 1
    App->>App: Produce Item 2
    Client->>Client: Process Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 2
    App->>App: Produce Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 2
    App->>Client: Send Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 3
    Note over App: Keeps producing...
    Note over Client: Keeps consuming...

flat_nodes.section_text:
In these cases, it's common to send " JSON Lines ", which is a format where you send one JSON object per line.

A response would have a content type of application/jsonl (instead of application/json ) and the body would be something like:

It's very similar to a JSON array (equivalent of a Python list), but instead of being wrapped in [] and having , between the items, it has one JSON object per line , they are separated by a new line character.
You could use this to stream data from an AI LLM service, from logs or telemetry , or from other types of data that can be structured in JSON items.

To stream JSON Lines with FastAPI you can, instead of using return in your path operation function , use yield to produce each item in turn.

If each JSON item you want to send back is of type Item (a Pydantic model) and it's an async function, you can declare the return type as AsyncIterable[Item] :

If you declare the return type, FastAPI will use it to validate the data, document it in OpenAPI, filter it, and serialize it using Pydantic.

You can also use regular def functions (without async ), and use yield the same way.

FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.

As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be Iterable[Item] :
You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will then use the jsonable_encoder to convert the data to something that can be serialized to JSON and then send it as JSON Lines.

FastAPI also has first-class support for Server-Sent Events (SSE), which are quite similar but with a couple of extra details. You can learn about them in the next chapter: Server-Sent Events (SSE) . 🤓

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0001.section_text: You could have a sequence of data that you would like to send in a " stream ", you could do it with JSON Lines .

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0002.section_text:
" Streaming " data means that your app will start sending data items to the client without waiting for the entire sequence of items to be ready.

So, it will send the first item, the client will receive and start processing it, and you might still be producing the next item.

It could even be an infinite stream, where you keep sending data.

code_blocks.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant App
    participant Client

    App->>App: Produce Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 1
    App->>App: Produce Item 2
    Client->>Client: Process Item 1
    App->>Client: Send Item 2
    App->>App: Produce Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 2
    App->>Client: Send Item 3
    Client->>Client: Process Item 3
    Note over App: Keeps producing...
    Note over Client: Keeps consuming...

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0003.section_text:
In these cases, it's common to send " JSON Lines ", which is a format where you send one JSON object per line.

A response would have a content type of application/jsonl (instead of application/json ) and the body would be something like:

It's very similar to a JSON array (equivalent of a Python list), but instead of being wrapped in [] and having , between the items, it has one JSON object per line , they are separated by a new line character.

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0004.section_text: You could use this to stream data from an AI LLM service, from logs or telemetry , or from other types of data that can be structured in JSON items.

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0005.section_text:
To stream JSON Lines with FastAPI you can, instead of using return in your path operation function , use yield to produce each item in turn.

If each JSON item you want to send back is of type Item (a Pydantic model) and it's an async function, you can declare the return type as AsyncIterable[Item] :

If you declare the return type, FastAPI will use it to validate the data, document it in OpenAPI, filter it, and serialize it using Pydantic.

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0006.section_text:
You can also use regular def functions (without async ), and use yield the same way.

FastAPI will make sure it's run correctly so that it doesn't block the event loop.

As in this case the function is not async, the right return type would be Iterable[Item] :

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0007.section_text: You can also omit the return type. FastAPI will then use the jsonable_encoder to convert the data to something that can be serialized to JSON and then send it as JSON Lines.

tutorial/stream-json-lines::section-0008.section_text: FastAPI also has first-class support for Server-Sent Events (SSE), which are quite similar but with a couple of extra details. You can learn about them in the next chapter: Server-Sent Events (SSE) . 🤓

section_text_parts: Added in FastAPI 0.134.0.
The important point is that your app will be able to produce each line in turn, while the client consumes the previous lines.
admonition_nodes.section_text: The important point is that your app will be able to produce each line in turn, while the client consumes the previous lines.

admonition_nodes.title: Technical Details
section_text_parts: Because each JSON object will be separated by a new line, they can't contain literal new line characters in their content, but they can contain escaped new lines ( \n ), which is part of the JSON standard.
But normally you won't have to worry about it, it's done automatically, continue reading. 🤓
admonition_nodes.section_text:
Because each JSON object will be separated by a new line, they can't contain literal new line characters in their content, but they can contain escaped new lines ( \n ), which is part of the JSON standard.

But normally you won't have to worry about it, it's done automatically, continue reading. 🤓

section_text_parts: If you want to stream binary data, for example video or audio, check the advanced guide: Stream Data .
admonition_nodes.section_text: If you want to stream binary data, for example video or audio, check the advanced guide: Stream Data .

section_text_parts: As Pydantic will serialize it in the Rust side, you will get much higher performance than if you don't declare a return type.
admonition_nodes.section_text: As Pydantic will serialize it in the Rust side, you will get much higher performance than if you don't declare a return type.
